refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall achieve and maintain operational availability of ≥0.97 (97%) over the customer-defined evaluation period, measured as the ratio of time the system is capable of performing its intended mission to total operational time, accounting for planned maintenance, unplanned downtime, and safety-driven shutdowns.
refined_fr.rationale: CR-NGHE-004 is a key performance indicator (KPI) that directly impacts customer satisfaction and mission success. Refining it explicitly defines the measurement basis (availability ratio), the target threshold (≥0.97), the evaluation period scope, and acknowledges that availability is a system-level emergent property dependent on vehicle reliability, teleoperation robustness, safety service responsiveness, and maintenance effectiveness. High safety criticality demands that availability not be achieved by masking failures but by genuine fault tolerance and rapid recovery.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Continuous telemetry logging of system state transitions (operational, degraded, maintenance, safety-hold, offline); statistical analysis of uptime/downtime over the agreed evaluation window; root-cause analysis of all downtime events; comparison against customer-defined baseline and acceptance criteria.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-004.1: The Vehicle subsystem shall maintain mechanical and electrical reliability such that unplanned downtime due to component failure does not exceed the availability budget allocation.
FR-NGHE-004.2: The Teleoperation subsystem shall maintain network connectivity and command/response latency within acceptable bounds such that tele-operator-induced unavailability is minimized.

FR-NGHE-004.3: The Safety Services subsystem shall detect and respond to hazardous conditions within defined response times, triggering safe shutdown or degraded-mode operation without cascading system loss.
FR-NGHE-004.4: The NGHE Solution shall implement predictive maintenance scheduling and fault diagnostics to reduce mean time to repair (MTTR) and extend mean time between failures (MTBF).

candidate_dps: DP-A: Implement redundancy (hardware and software) across critical subsystems (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services) with automatic failover and health monitoring to mask transient faults and reduce downtime.

DP-B: Establish a comprehensive predictive maintenance and diagnostics framework that continuously monitors system health via telemetry, predicts failures before they occur, and schedules maintenance to minimize unplanned downtime.

DP-C: Design the NGHE Solution as a fault-tolerant architecture with graceful degradation modes, where non-critical subsystems can be isolated without loss of core mission capability, combined with rapid recovery protocols.

DP-D: Implement a closed-loop availability management system that tracks real-time availability metrics, triggers corrective actions (maintenance, failover, mode switching), and provides visibility to stakeholders via dashboards.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-Integrated: Combine DP-A (redundancy and failover), DP-B (predictive maintenance), and DP-C (graceful degradation) into a unified fault-tolerant architecture, with DP-D (closed-loop availability management) as the orchestration and visibility layer. This integrated approach addresses availability holistically: preventing failures (DP-B), masking failures when they occur (DP-A), maintaining mission capability under degradation (DP-C), and enabling rapid response (DP-D).

potential_sources: Redundancy (DP-A) and predictive maintenance (DP-B) are complementary: redundancy provides immediate fault masking while predictive maintenance reduces the frequency of failures requiring failover.
Graceful degradation (DP-C) and closed-loop management (DP-D) are tightly coupled: DP-D must detect degradation triggers and orchestrate mode transitions defined in DP-C.
All four DPs depend on continuous telemetry and health monitoring; this is a shared dependency but not a coupling defect if telemetry is treated as a foundational service.
coupling_check.notes: The integrated DP is not orthogonal but is justified by the high safety criticality and the emergent nature of availability. Decoupling would require separate availability targets for each subsystem, which is less effective than system-level orchestration. Coupling is acceptable if interfaces between redundancy, maintenance, degradation, and management are clearly defined.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Redundancy can be implemented cost-effectively within project budget and weight/power constraints.
Assumption: Predictive maintenance algorithms can be trained on sufficient historical failure data or validated via accelerated testing.
Assumption: Network connectivity for telemetry is reliable enough to support real-time health monitoring; if network is unreliable, on-board diagnostics must compensate.
Risk: Over-reliance on redundancy may mask underlying design flaws; root-cause analysis and design improvements must accompany redundancy deployment.
Risk: Graceful degradation modes may not be acceptable to the operator or customer if mission capability is significantly reduced; acceptance criteria for degraded modes must be negotiated.
Risk: Closed-loop management system itself becomes a single point of failure if not designed with high availability; management system must be distributed or have fallback logic.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Clarify the availability budget allocation across Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services subsystems (e.g., Vehicle 0.985, Teleoperation 0.99, Safety Services 0.995). This enables each subsystem team to design to a specific target rather than the system-level 0.97, ensuring that the integrated system meets the overall KPI. Additionally, define the acceptable degradation modes and their impact on mission effectiveness; this informs the design of DP-C.

im_boundary.name: NGHE Solution
im_boundary.scope: The NGHE Solution module is the top-level system integration boundary that orchestrates availability across all subsystems (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services). It owns the closed-loop availability management system (DP-D), the fault-tolerance orchestration logic, and the interfaces that expose availability metrics and control signals to external stakeholders (Site Manager, Safety Manager, Maintenance/Quality). The NGHE Solution does not own the Vehicle, Teleoperation, or Safety Services subsystems directly but coordinates their health, failover, and degradation modes.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Inbound health and status telemetry from Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services subsystems; includes component health indicators, failure predictions, and real-time availability metrics. Used by NGHE Solution to drive closed-loop availability management.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Bidirectional mission tasking and mode control; NGHE Solution receives mission requests from Site Manager/Tele-operator and may modify or defer tasking based on availability state (e.g., defer non-critical tasks during maintenance windows or degraded modes).

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Outbound safety alerts and availability status notifications to Safety Manager and Site Manager; includes alerts for predicted failures, graceful degradation triggers, and recovery status.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Underlying network connectivity for all telemetry, tasking, and safety alert exchanges; NGHE Solution depends on Network Link availability and may trigger fallback modes if network is degraded.

module_containment_scores.notes: NGHE Solution is the natural owner of the integrated availability management system (DP-D) and the orchestration logic for redundancy, predictive maintenance, and graceful degradation. The closed-loop management, fault-tolerance coordination, and stakeholder visibility are system-level concerns that belong at the top level. Minimal diffusion: only the telemetry aggregation and mode-switching logic are contained here; detailed subsystem health algorithms remain in Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services.

Vehicle owns its own mechanical and electrical reliability (DP-A redundancy within the vehicle), predictive maintenance for vehicle-specific components (DP-B), and graceful degradation modes for vehicle subsystems (DP-C). However, Vehicle does not own the system-level availability orchestration or the integration with Teleoperation and Safety Services; this diffusion is appropriate because Vehicle is a subsystem, not the system integrator.

Teleoperation owns its own network redundancy, failover to backup communication links (DP-A), and predictive maintenance for communication hardware (DP-B). Teleoperation may implement graceful degradation (e.g., reduced command frequency, increased latency tolerance) but does not own the system-level availability orchestration. Diffusion is expected and acceptable.

Safety Services owns the detection and response logic for hazardous conditions (DP-C graceful degradation via safe shutdown), predictive maintenance for safety-critical sensors (DP-B), and redundancy in safety-critical circuits (DP-A). Safety Services has higher containment because safety is a cross-cutting concern that must be tightly integrated. However, Safety Services does not own the system-level availability orchestration; it provides inputs (safety alerts) to NGHE Solution, which coordinates the response.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The NGHE Solution module should own the integrated availability management system because: (1) Availability is a system-level emergent property that cannot be achieved by any single subsystem in isolation; (2) The closed-loop management, orchestration of redundancy/failover/degradation, and stakeholder visibility are inherently cross-cutting concerns; (3) NGHE Solution has the authority and visibility to coordinate trade-offs between subsystems (e.g., deferring non-critical teleoperation tasks to prioritize vehicle maintenance); (4) The four known interfaces (Telemetry, Tasking, SafetyAlert, Network Link) naturally converge at the NGHE Solution level, making it the logical integration point.
containment_risks: Risk: NGHE Solution may become a bottleneck or single point of failure if the closed-loop management system is not itself designed with high availability (e.g., distributed state, fallback logic, graceful degradation of management functions).

Risk: Subsystems (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services) may resist providing detailed health telemetry or accepting mode-switching commands from NGHE Solution if ownership boundaries are not clearly defined; interface contracts must be explicit.

Risk: The integration of redundancy, predictive maintenance, graceful degradation, and closed-loop management is complex; implementation may diffuse across multiple code modules or physical components within NGHE Solution, making it difficult to verify and maintain.
Risk: If Network Link is unreliable, the closed-loop management system may not receive timely telemetry or may not be able to send control signals; fallback logic must be pre-defined and tested.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Define explicit interface contracts between NGHE Solution and each subsystem (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services) specifying: (1) Telemetry schema and update frequency; (2) Tasking/mode-switching commands and response times; (3) SafetyAlert triggers and escalation procedures; (4) Fallback behavior if Network Link is degraded. Additionally, decompose the NGHE Solution module into sub-modules (e.g., AvailabilityMonitor, FaultOrchestrator, MaintenanceScheduler, DegradationController) to manage complexity and enable independent testing and ownership.

fr_dp_gate.reason: The refined FR (achieve ≥0.97 availability over the evaluation period) is directly addressed by the selected DP (integrated fault-tolerance architecture combining redundancy, predictive maintenance, graceful degradation, and closed-loop management). The DP is logically sound and feasible given the constraints. The coupling between the four DP components is acceptable because they are complementary and necessary for a holistic availability solution. The FR is measurable (availability ratio), and the DP is implementable (redundancy, diagnostics, and management systems are well-established technologies).

diffusion_risks: The integrated DP is assigned to NGHE Solution, which is the correct system-level owner. However, the implementation of the DP will necessarily diffuse into subsystems (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services) because each subsystem must implement its own redundancy, predictive maintenance, and graceful degradation. This diffusion is expected and acceptable if interface contracts are clear.

The closed-loop management system (DP-D) is owned by NGHE Solution but depends on telemetry from all subsystems and must send control signals to all subsystems. If the management system is implemented as a monolithic component, it may become a bottleneck; distributed or hierarchical management is recommended.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: NGHE Solution is the appropriate IM boundary for the integrated availability management system. The module has high containment (0.95) for the orchestration and visibility logic, with acceptable diffusion (0.05) to subsystems for detailed health algorithms. The four known interfaces (Telemetry, Tasking, SafetyAlert, Network Link) are all present and correctly assigned. No new interfaces are required. The ownership boundary is clear: NGHE Solution owns the system-level availability strategy and orchestration; subsystems own their own reliability and maintenance strategies.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Verification of the refined FR (≥0.97 availability) is feasible via continuous telemetry logging, statistical analysis of uptime/downtime, and root-cause analysis of all downtime events. The NGHE Solution module will own the availability monitoring and reporting infrastructure, making it straightforward to collect and analyze availability data. Acceptance testing can be conducted over the customer-defined evaluation period (e.g., 6 months, 1 year) with clear pass/fail criteria.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: The allocation of the FR to the NGHE Solution IM is clear. The NGHE Solution is responsible for achieving the ≥0.97 availability target by orchestrating the reliability, maintenance, and safety services of all subsystems. Each subsystem (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services) has a derived availability target (to be defined in the recommended revision) that contributes to the system-level target. The allocation is hierarchical and traceable.

recommended_revisions: Define explicit availability budget allocations for Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services subsystems (e.g., Vehicle 0.985, Teleoperation 0.99, Safety Services 0.995) to ensure that the integrated system meets the ≥0.97 target. This enables each subsystem to design to a specific, measurable goal.

Specify the acceptable degradation modes and their impact on mission effectiveness (e.g., reduced speed, reduced precision, reduced range). This informs the design of graceful degradation and helps the customer understand the trade-offs between availability and mission capability.

Define the fallback behavior of the NGHE Solution if Network Link is degraded or unavailable. For example, the system may switch to on-board diagnostics and pre-defined failover logic without relying on real-time telemetry from the cloud or remote management center.

Decompose the NGHE Solution module into sub-modules (AvailabilityMonitor, FaultOrchestrator, MaintenanceScheduler, DegradationController) to manage complexity and enable independent testing and ownership. Define the interfaces between these sub-modules.

Establish a continuous improvement process for the availability management system, including regular review of failure data, refinement of predictive maintenance algorithms, and updates to graceful degradation modes based on operational experience.

decision.justification: The refined FR (CR-NGHE-004) is a system-level KPI that is not atomic and requires further decomposition into sub-FRs that address specific availability drivers: vehicle reliability, teleoperation robustness, safety service responsiveness, and maintenance effectiveness. The selected DP (integrated fault-tolerance architecture) is sound and feasible, but its implementation requires detailed design at the subsystem level. The assigned IM (NGHE Solution) is the correct system-level owner, but the orchestration logic and interfaces must be further refined through decomposition of the sub-FRs. Proceeding to the next level of functional decomposition will clarify the detailed requirements for each subsystem and enable more precise DP selection and IM assignment at the subsystem level.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-NGHE-004.1: The Vehicle subsystem shall maintain mechanical and electrical reliability such that unplanned downtime due to component failure does not exceed the availability budget allocation (e.g., ≤0.015 downtime fraction).

FR-NGHE-004.2: The Teleoperation subsystem shall maintain network connectivity and command/response latency within acceptable bounds such that tele-operator-induced unavailability does not exceed the availability budget allocation (e.g., ≤0.010 downtime fraction).

FR-NGHE-004.3: The Safety Services subsystem shall detect and respond to hazardous conditions within defined response times, triggering safe shutdown or degraded-mode operation without cascading system loss, such that safety-driven unavailability does not exceed the availability budget allocation (e.g., ≤0.005 downtime fraction).

FR-NGHE-004.4: The NGHE Solution shall implement predictive maintenance scheduling and fault diagnostics to reduce mean time to repair (MTTR) and extend mean time between failures (MTBF), such that maintenance-driven unavailability does not exceed the availability budget allocation (e.g., ≤0.010 downtime fraction).

assumptions: Availability is measured as the ratio of time the system is capable of performing its intended mission to total operational time, excluding planned maintenance windows.
The customer-defined evaluation period is at least 3 months to ensure statistical significance of availability measurements.
Redundancy can be implemented cost-effectively within project budget and weight/power constraints; detailed trade-off analysis is required.
Predictive maintenance algorithms can be trained on sufficient historical failure data or validated via accelerated testing; data collection and algorithm development are in-scope.
Network connectivity for telemetry is reliable enough to support real-time health monitoring; if network is unreliable, on-board diagnostics and fallback logic must compensate.
Graceful degradation modes are acceptable to the operator and customer; acceptance criteria for degraded modes must be negotiated with stakeholders.
The closed-loop availability management system is itself designed with high availability (e.g., distributed state, fallback logic, graceful degradation of management functions).
Each subsystem (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services) can be assigned a specific availability target that contributes to the system-level ≥0.97 target.
open_risks: Risk: Over-reliance on redundancy may mask underlying design flaws; root-cause analysis and design improvements must accompany redundancy deployment.
Risk: Graceful degradation modes may not be acceptable to the operator or customer if mission capability is significantly reduced; acceptance criteria for degraded modes must be negotiated.
Risk: Closed-loop management system itself becomes a single point of failure if not designed with high availability; management system must be distributed or have fallback logic.
Risk: NGHE Solution may become a bottleneck or single point of failure if the closed-loop management system is not itself designed with high availability.
Risk: Subsystems may resist providing detailed health telemetry or accepting mode-switching commands from NGHE Solution if ownership boundaries are not clearly defined.

Risk: The integration of redundancy, predictive maintenance, graceful degradation, and closed-loop management is complex; implementation may diffuse across multiple code modules or physical components, making it difficult to verify and maintain.
Risk: If Network Link is unreliable, the closed-loop management system may not receive timely telemetry or may not be able to send control signals; fallback logic must be pre-defined and tested.
Risk: Availability targets for subsystems may be infeasible or may require excessive redundancy; trade-off analysis and feasibility studies are required.
evidence_needed: Historical failure data for similar equipment or subsystems to validate predictive maintenance algorithms and estimate MTBF/MTTR.
Network reliability data for the teleoperation link to assess the feasibility of real-time telemetry and closed-loop management.
Cost and weight/power trade-off analysis for redundancy options (e.g., dual motors, dual communication links, dual safety sensors).
Acceptance criteria and stakeholder feedback on graceful degradation modes and their impact on mission effectiveness.
Detailed design and testing results for the closed-loop availability management system, including failure modes and fallback logic.
Subsystem-level availability targets and design specifications derived from the system-level ≥0.97 target.
Operational data from field trials or pilot deployments to validate availability predictions and refine maintenance schedules.
Customer-defined evaluation period and acceptance criteria for the availability KPI.
